item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was about the Q value (like the one used in nuclear physics) going haywire or something but boy i was wrong. Anyway very creative writing which nobody other than you could think about. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I too would like to know what happens if you put both signs on something! And it does sound like something they would test…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This oddly reminds me of that spongebob episode where they think Mr.Krabs is a robot.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely worth the read. It's still a little confusing what is defined as "humanity." I understand it's a very subjective thing, but if someone is exposed to the beta will they still be perceived as a living thing such as say a dog? Or will they be considered say an inanimate pointless object? I find it hard to believe most people would instantly kill another living creature. Who knows perhaps it's just me. Anyways, definitely gets an upvote. love the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ERMAHGERD!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slap Q on 053.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're biological humans that lack humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So if humanity includes "humans who don't smile", then "humans who smile" aren't humans - except the very statement categorizes them as humans. I feel like this paradox is a little too prominent to go unaddressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh I like this one. An SCP created by the foundation that can change a persons perception of humanity, thats an incredibly useful item for the foundation. Of course, if another group were to acquire this item…. that could be a problem….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, may I ask two questions?
1) Why anyone in-universe would assign scp-status to this project? Its even less anomalous, than most memes, known to Foundation (cause this one was made by itself), and it does nothing unless used by it on global scale. Also you can mess-up someones mind pretty well (even in a similar manner) with common drugs. Why ain't they on the list, then?
2) I really can't find enough creepyness in this. Don't get me wrong the writing is good and nice to read, but the whole concept is just a "Foundation is cruel and dehumanizing" thrown in the face of reader without any additional elaboration on topic. And this trope has been for years overdone so much that the entire article starts to summarise in something like "yo Foundation is so cruel it has tags to determine who's human and who's not". So, where to be creeped out, then?

So, for now, it's a downvote from me. Still like your other work, though)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, Accelerando.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this qualifies as a memetic kill agent. Like, this seems like a combination of an HCF command for the brain, and a 1=0 type deal.

If your counter-meme kills everything, I don't think it's working.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably the easiest counter-meme I've ever developed.

+ Collapsed your image for you. - Accel
- hide

There. Now nothing is or isn't human, because everything is and isn't human. Problem solved. Everybody get back to work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll just leave this here, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is all very, very good. I love how you used elission in the final log to twist my expectations back and forth and end in a really horrific place. Also that you used proper scientific methodology.

I also have this image in my head of certain people walking around the Foundation with Q and = cards in their pockets, ready.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is, very much so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's working as intended, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The collapsible text is broken. +1 anyway
oh nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me very strongly of SCP-2025 but I liked that and I like this. The note at the end was a perfect addition: I love pieces which consider how the Foundation interacts with SCPs. I would have enjoyed a bit more in the way of demonstration of the effects, but that perhaps would have encroached too much on 2025's territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A short and simple, yet interesting SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think "leaves me wanting more" is about what I was aiming for with those — I could go into the nitty-gritty about how it functions under different circumstances, and those tests could theoretically all be worth reading in a vacuum, but adding too much more would detract from the article as a whole. So basically, your assessment is right.

2122 isn't explicitly connected to 3021, nor vice versa. They just deal with similar themes, use similar literary gimmicks, and are structured similarly — to the point where I consider them companion pieces. Is it possible that 3021 is derived in some respect from 2122, or vice versa? Yes, absolutely, and that's a cool interpretation. But it's not something I'll claim as true.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is some seriously interesting stuff, and the test logs definitely leave me wanting more, but too much could bloat the article up I suppose.

+ Possible spoilers?
- Oh god I hope I'm using collapsibles right

And after reading 2122, is this skip supposed to be 2122-2…? I noticed 2122-2 was only mentioned in the 2122 article a single time, as something with potential "applications", so I'm wondering if 3021 is a more fleshed out version of it. It would certainly fit with the Foundation's indoctrination goals, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scantron does it yet again. What a neat concept and how it applies to Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last bit really nailed this for me. Yes, the Foundation has memetics that can invasively redefine how you view humanity. Yes, they COULD use these memetics to brainwash personnel into seeing D-Class and humanoid skips as nonhuman. But they don't, because they don't even need to. That's both horrifying and disturbingly reassuring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alpha would be useful if you wanted to say introduce sapient AIs or were alien invaders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's not literate w/r/t social science, I'm not getting as much out of college as I thought I was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a treat to read. The concept is delightfully creepy, in that it highlights one of the Foundation's more prominent aspects - their (arguably necessary) callous understanding of human life and its expenditure. Providing them with the power to define on a dime what qualifies as human and what deserves to die is pretty horrifying, and the depth you go to exploring this exploits the idea well. As for the technical stuff - there's little to say. Tone was spot on, which I think was really necessary in this case, considering the article's content. Spag errors were absent, the piece was tight, and as far as reconciling IC parascience with OOC social mechanics - I think it's literate? In any case, I bought the interactions, and in the end I guess that's what's important.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That testing log was glorious, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, but I'm not sure I get it. Or something. I mean, the point is a lack of signifiers won't preclude humanity or otherwise in the viewer's mind, but I was sort of expecting that they would be common things one might see at random. So I guess the point is to train up mindless killers or something, using Signifier Beta? Interesting how the subjects generalize humanity or lack thereof, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Believing something is a human and treating it as such can be two very different things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what happens if you use both Q and = at the same time ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure what it's talking about. * still upvotes*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see the similarities, though clearly that one took humanity-play in a much sillier direction.

This is sort of a spiritual successor to 2122. I definitely imagine these two existing in the same universe, and having overlapping personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of the cone of humanity, but the Will Smith test gave a wow wow factor.

ETA: And if it's linked to 2122, then yikes do both this and that get extra-creepy. Jesus.

read scp-2122 it's good
